author: @Kargoneth
text: Methanol, methyl hydrate, wood alcohol, and all if its other synonyms can make it a but tricky to find, but it is a decent fuel. It can burn with a wide variety of oxygen levels. It can be doused quickly via dilution with water. Just be careful when pouring it. It has very low viscosity and very low surface tension. It spills relatively easily, often runs down the side of the jug, and is liable to drip on the ground while being poured. Being transparent and colourless does not do a person anyone any favours if it is spilled on certain surfaces.

replies.author: @Kargoneth
replies.text: *all of its synonyms

author: @Kargoneth
text: I suspect that, ideally, you'd want to use a plant-based-fibre sock (cotton, hemp, etc). Less carcinogenic and poisonous fumes (nevermind melted residues) than a synthetic-plastic-fibre (polyester, nylon, etc) sock and less stench than a fur-based-fibre (wool, felted hair, etc) sock. Of course, if the only available alternative to using synthetic-plastic-fibres is hypothermia, then use the synthetic-plastic-fibre sock!

text: Robert mentioned here that carbon felt is difficult to find. Ironically, you probably already have some carbon felt you could use in an emergency situation. author: @genesturby9247
text: Hummmm. 🤔.  Back in the old days ( early 1970s ) when I worked on the British Columbia Railway we would heat our Speeder ( rail scooter ) with methyl hydrate...  We used a one gallon tin can  with fiberglass insulation stuffed into it so would fill the bottom 3 inches or so, that was our wick .  Then we would pour in the methyl hydrate and light it.  It burned nice and clean and provided heat for a fair while.  That winter we burned up a 45 gallon barrel of the stuff. When it ran out we switched over to propane and a torch.  We only had one small misshap when we caught the Speeder's canvas door on 🔥 fire.  😮😊.

replies.author: @pedroi9008
replies.text: Hello. I was just passing through when I read your question. I don't have the answer you're asking for, but I'd think that you should be looking for a measurement in volume, such as cubic feet, rather than square feet. Say, for example, a ten by ten feet room has an area of a hundred square feet, but the volume of air to be heated would depend on the height of the ceiling. A 10'x10' room with 10' high ceiling would get heated a lot quicker than a 10'x10' room with 12 or more feet in height. I live South of the border near San Diego, Ca. and I don't know exactly what the temperature is in my room right now, but I've used an upside-down  terracotta pot with three or four candles underneath inside a small 10'x10' room with 9 or 10 feet high ceiling, and the difference can be felt. Now, that methanol and sock heater looks a lot more fierce than a pot and some candles. I hope you get your doubts cleared by someone more knowledgable than me!

author: @Chimonger1
text:
Love your videos!  
🤔Umm…is carbon felt wick, same as carbon felt welding blanket/shield??
Because, if it is, Amazon has , many sizes, & not much money.  Or a local store selling welding supplies likely has it, too. 
Also, considering stability…
Someone suggested magnets on bottom of the can reservoir, to magnet it in place. 
But they worried about magnet degradation…
Let’s THINK about logistics of parts & function: 
🤔 If magnets are on the can-base, & it got hot enuf to degrade magnets…
…wouldn’t that kind of heat, at minimum, increase the gasification of the alcohol in the can reservoir, which would increase flame, & decrease burn-time…..if not explosion (overheating would have to happen pretty fast to do that, though)? 
🤔I’d think, having the small air gap  the can, formed by the magnets, could also (for a certain amount) help keep the can reservoir a bit cooler? (Not that it gets too hot…?)
🤔And, one could use a piece of flat metal w/center hole in it, to form a top-shield around top of can, to reflect flame heat up, instead of letting any go down around the can? 
🤔IF concerned about the magnets, maybe Neodymium ones last longer? 
🤔IF concerned about the magnets, then instead, maybe use a slightly larger ring than the can-base—maybe a simple metal cookie cutter—attached, centered, where the reservoir can is to sit-into, to make sure the heater assembly cannot tip or move?  That could be stuck in place using high-temp stove caulk? 
🤔Is there a decent safe way to extinguish one of these?  …Maybe another piece of curved sheet metal to make a fairly tight-fitted front door, which could cut-off O2 getting into the stove, snuffing the flame?   …Or an Internally mounted damper w/handle outside of can to operate it? 
This heater is a great survival idea!   
Even better, using recycled materials as much as one is able. 
Countable less toxic emissions than, say, propane.   & it’s limited batch use, important to prevent unattended use. 
Alcohol burners have been used in boats, for Ages!…& one can ferment their own alcohol, if they know how & have the materials. 
There are recycled tin can alcohol cookers, too…saw videos about making those, too…those make less heat, shorter burn-times, but for some, might be safer?

author: @hubrisnaut
text: We have a couple of types of kerosene heaters sold in retail stores here in the US. They are all require a "tip over" safety mechanism. They use a duel tank system and a weighted "trigger" attached to a spring loaded wick holder that retracts to accomplish this. It would be awesome if some enterprising person could accomplish a similar DIY solution. I remember my dad had a Coleman brand camping tent heater. I have no idea how it worked.

replies.author: @__-fm5qv
replies.text: I guess what you could do is just have it in a sufficently tall base structure, with some width at the top, and have the whole burner hang from the top. That way if it falls over the actual burner remains upright, even if the stand is on its side. And by some width I mean it would have to be wider at the top than the burner is long so it can swing a full 90 degrees to the side if necessary. A bit of a cumbersome solution, and not as safe as compartmentalising the fuel properly but it's safer than nothing.

replies.author: @hubrisnaut
replies.text: @__-fm5qv I love DIY things but It's probably best just to buy a code safety rated heater. It makes no sense to risk burning down your house or killing someone to save a few dollars. I bought a kerosene heater for $50 on spring sale at Tractor Supply 6-7 years ago. I'm sure they cost more now but I need a new one. Mine started shutting itself off because of gear wear on the spring loaded wick adjuster. It would trigger the safely shut off when the gears slipped. Methanol or ethanol fuel is to expensive to heat your home with anyway. It's fine for camping hikes outdoors to boil water because you don't use much, just a couple ounces at a time . I made one of those tiny soda can ones to see if I could. Frankly, where I live you don't need a camp stove. We just build wood fires. If there is a fire ban, no problem. I have 2 lightweight propane stoves that use the 1 pound tanks available at almost any corner store. (I might build a refiller) One is a compact single burner that I bought at a yard sale for $5, still in the box. The 2 burner one I got for free at a local recycling center. My sadly departed master craftsman uncle (miss you Larry), who hand built Ice racing boats for fun, gave me good advice, with proof, which I take into account till this day, "don't try to reinvent the wheel, It's a waste of time". The path of least resistance has usually been tread before. For sure, innovation is possible but be wise about what you put your energy into. Look into new possibilities.

author: @8ank3r
text: I love this heater.  I made one but I used NO WICK.  I simply put a smaller can with no top in the outside can.  A little fuel in the small can gets lit and as the little can floats on the fuel in the big can it heats/gassifies the fuel in the large can, which passes out the gap between the cans and burns.  Hope you like the super easy no wick version.  It's almost like a penny stove.

replies.text: Trying to picture this....  So 2 cans.  The outside larger can has the top removed only.  The smaller inside can also has only the top removed and floats on the liquid -at a  constant level.  I am assuming the mesh (what size mesh is everyone using) is attached to the inside floating can.  Maybe safety wire would work if wound it around tightly.  The fuel is always in contact with the mesh and wicks up the mesh which is between the 2 cans.  Correct?  If so, does the fuel wick up high enough when the fuel is low in the outside can and the inside can is floating low?

replies.author: @8ank3r
replies.text: @slrs3908 yes and it stays hot and in turn heats the fuel in the large can. The mesh is on the outside of the larger can so that the inner can is free to move up and down with the fuel level.  A little fuel is put into the inner can and lit to get the gassification of the main fuel in the larger started. No wicking only gassification of the fuel in the bigger can which floats up the space between the two cans and ignites

author: @robertzeurunkl8401
text: So, I built this (from the original video, with proper carbon wick which I ordered from Amazon) and lit it in my small (outbuilding) office.  About 12 feet by 9 feet in dimension.  Curious about it's fumes, I decided to put my Carbon Monoxide alarm next to it, and within 20 minutes, the alarm went off with CO levels already at 200 ppm and climbing fast.  The only deviation from this video is that I burned denatured alcohol instead of methanol.  Would methanol actually make any difference?  I opened the door and let the room air out to let it continue to burn, but 8oz of alcohol only burned for about an hour.  Not "4 hours".  The fit between the inner and outer can was exactly the thickness of the carbon wick, so it was a good fit.  Does, perhaps, denatured alcohol just burn up faster than methanol?  I did read online that denatured alcohol is more energy dense, and hotter, than methanol, so maybe it burns faster?

text: Ingenious, but I would be cautious about burning textiles indoors. 100% wool is probably the safest option. author: @pacman4568
text: Two small things. 1 What is the sock made of? 2 I tried something like this with the carbon wick, I forgot to punch a small hole in the top of the inner can. Very soon after, the whole thing went POP! And almost exploded in a giant fireball, in my kitchen. The inner can shot out and hit the top of the burner, taking the top mesh cover with it. Thank god it did not tip over. Pressure built up inside the inner can and the pool of fuel ignited. I did not see you make a small pinhole in the top of an inner can this time. So, tell me, can you do without the inner can and its pinhole? In my tests, 300ml of methanol burnt for 40-45 mins. With this, you did away with the top of the inner can. replies.author: @pa_maj.MARTINI-van-MAN
replies.text:
That's actually the third time now that I've heard of this happen Packman. 
He did say on one video if I remember correctly that a pinhole wasn't needed but personally I think it's safer with a larger hole around a 25mm and also fill the inside can with yellow fiberglass insulation or fiberglass cloth, even those SS household scrubbing pads. 
A circle of the SS gauze fitted from underneath to dress the hole to make it look tidy.
This will burn very little fuel in the centre and will not create a vapour chamber to blow the can out, a 15mm hole may even be better but it's all experimental of-course Packman. 
I have found while trying other experiments that to get Good heat, the fuel goes much quicker than I'd like it to and slowing the burn rate down too much to get a longer burn produces little heat.
There's been no win win for me, all fuel is expensive unless it's free.

I'm now using Tealight candles between my seat and my footstool inside a large soup/stew pot (right way up) to warm the back of my legs until my halogen heater arrives. 
I've been doing this for around five days now rather than give the gas company my money heating the whole house.
I had 500 Tealight candles I never used that I got for £5.00 at B&Q years ago.
My food grade Ethanol for making tinctures is too expensive to be burning for heat.
It was great to experiment a little with though to work out burn rates, I'll not be buying even the cheaper non food grade Ethanol for this as it's also just not cost effective for me.
Probably OK for an emergency situation which is fair enough.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
@peterjohnston4472 I had only thought being able to put it out would be a good idea.  As to how, maybe someone might present a better idea but my first thought would be to use another can that is taller than the mantle, like maybe a hair spray can or lysol can.

The can must also be able to somewhat loosly slide over the mantle. Not too tight nor too loose. And remember the mantal will have  expanded a little while it's hot. 

If you can't find a suitable diameter can then you might use two cans. To do so you'll need to 
1) know the circumfrence of the mantle, maybe using a string. And add a milimeter or two. 
2) Then divide that by four to get 1/4th the circumference.  
3) Then take two round steel hair spray cans or lysol steel cans and cut down the length of each can to get two curled sheet from the cans.
4) Now first mark the center of the curve in the can top and bottom. 
5) Then measure out 1/4 the circumfrence from before on each side of your sheets, and make a new mark on each side of your first mark from top to bottom of each sheet.
6) Then take two block of wood longer than each sheet is tall and place them together with the steel  sheet in between such that the outside marked lines from before line up with the pieces of wood going from the bottom mark to the top mark.
7) Screw the pieces of wood together and use it as a metal break used to put clean sharp bends in the sheet metal from the cans, and put an 90 degree outward bend from the sheet's curl on each side of both sheets. 
8) Then you can bring the two curled sheets with the outside bent tabs together forming a circle inside, and  use vise grips to hold the tabs so that you may drill two holes on each  outward bent tab, and secure each side with 2 short bolts and nuts.

replies.author: @pa_maj.MARTINI-van-MAN
replies.text:
Ethanol, isopropanol and methanol can be put out with water if enough water is used. I'll use Ethanol as the example to keep things simple. Ethanol and water mix. The Ethanol will be over diluted with the water to a point of over dilution (not enough Ethanol in the mix) to the point it won't be flammable anymore as water is not flammable.
You Can Not do this with petrol/gasoline or oil fires.
I have experimented putting Ethanol out with water and it was quick and easy, you could too, in your garden to get a real world experience and understanding before  lighting one of these in a house or shed; a hand's on safety test.
I hope this helps Peter 👍Oh' smothering the lot with a really damp cotton towel or bed sheet will work too.

There is a school of thought that endlessly pointing out dangers and removing those dangers from people is actually dangerous. People become dependant on having them pointed out to them rather than working out the dangers for themselves! A bit like Mountain Climbing being safe Because it's dangerous (obviously so). Mountain climbers tend to take precautions. But how can you chose your precautions if you are never exposed to risk?
I am a proponent of telling kids that radiators are hot (maybe less so now though) and not to touch! Then when the kid eventually does touch it burns and they learn 2 things. 1. Don't touch hot radiators. 2. When someone says "Don't Touch!" it might be a good idea to follow the advice.

So Hot things burn and Sharp things can cut ...... Who'd of thunk it?

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
Wool has far better heat resistant properties than cotton.  Such as a combustion temp of 1100* F.  It is also not easy to ignite and tends to self extinguish.   Cotton on the other hand is readily flammable unless treated with baking soda or the like.  Cotton will start to burn/catch on fire at around 410* F.  

    Wool will smell a lot though once it starts burning, being made of protein vs cellulose.

Some lamps or burners that use pressure pump to move fuel, should have a pressure valve for safety and more fancy ones could have a check valve for fuel supply line to a burner. replies.author: @ogi22
replies.text:
It's a general rule of chemistry - liquids or solids don't burn (at least not easy), with some exceptions of compounds with oxydizers in them, but you should stay off those things if you just want to heat your room, not to remove it from the face of the earth. 
First you need to pump enough energy (in the form of heat) into your fuel, so it is ready to evaporate and combine with oxygen. But heating a can full of flammable substance like alcohol or gasoline would be unwise. Beleve me i tried it as a kid and i lost my eyebrows and a fringe 😆And i felt soooooo smart back then because i boiled alcohol just in a tiny bottle cap. But the flame was awesome! 🤩
That's why ancient people invented a wick - a substance that is able to "suck" and transport flammable fuel and to heat just a portion of it. Wick also gives more surface area for fuel to evaporate.
So if you dump a piece of natural rope in a bowl of oil, you have a somewhat safe way to have controlled flame. Or if you find such very old lamp, you could try rubbing it and hope for a djin to appear😜

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
You'll want to stick with natural materials as polyester, nylon, and polypropylene melts when heated.  Cotton etc doesn't melt, but burns, but it takes higher temps to get it there.  Wool has the highest heat resistant properties of common clothing materials.  Such as a combustion temp of around 1100* F, which is higher than kevlar and nomex--two materials considered and used as heat and fire resistant materials (firefighters, etc) .  

   But far better than any of the above are things like fiberglass clothe, rockwool insulation, ceramic fiber insulation, or pure carbon felt.
